text: Laparoscopic colectomy for T-for-B sigmoid colon carcinoma invading the bladder Laparoscopic surgery for PT-for-colon cancer is safe and feasible Any oncological outcomes are acceptable The aim of this video is to demonstrate a laparoscopic treatment for T-for-B sigmoid colon cancer invading part of the bladder The patient was a fifty-six-year-old female who presented abdominal pain and hematochesia for two months. Colonoscopy revealed occlusive mass and sigmoid colon and biopsy showed adenocarcinoma. Abdominal CT and MRI revealed the tumor was located at the lower sigmoid colon and posterior wall of the bladder was invaded. Cystoscope showed that the bladder mucosa was smooth and the bladder triangle was uninvaded. The operative time was one hundred thirty-five minutes, with an estimated blood loss of forty milliliters, and the post-operative hospital stay was seven days. The final pathological staging was PT-IV, BN-I, BM-Zero.

Laparoscopic colectomy for T-for-B sigmoid colon carcinoma invading the bladder Laparoscopic surgery for PT-for-colon cancer is safe and feasible Any oncological outcomes are acceptable The aim of this video is to demonstrate a laparoscopic treatment for T-for-B sigmoid colon cancer invading part of the bladder The patient was a fifty-six-year-old female who presented abdominal pain and hematochesia for two months. Colonoscopy revealed occlusive mass and sigmoid colon and biopsy showed adenocarcinoma. Abdominal CT and MRI revealed the tumor was located at the lower sigmoid colon and posterior wall of the bladder was invaded. Cystoscope showed that the bladder mucosa was smooth and the bladder triangle was uninvaded. The operative time was one hundred thirty-five minutes, with an estimated blood loss of forty milliliters, and the post-operative hospital stay was seven days. The final pathological staging was PT-IV, BN-I, BM-Zero. After exploring the abdominal cavity, the posterior wall of the bladder was incised one centimeter away from the tumor. Attention was paid to the integrity of the tumor and no injury to the bladder triangle was confirmed. The muscular and mucosal layers of the bladder were sutured with Then peritoneum was running sutured to embed the muscular layer.

The surgeon incises the posterior wall of the bladder one centimeter away from the tumor, ensuring the integrity of the tumor and avoiding injury to the bladder triangle. The muscular and mucosal layers of the bladder are sutured, followed by a running suture of the peritoneum to embed the muscular layer.

The surgical system lifts the inferior mesenteric artery, and the peritoneum is incised from the mesangial root of the right sigmoid colon to expose the space. The surgeon extends the space, exposing and protecting the hypogastric nerve. The posterior rectal space is separated, and the inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and ligated at its root. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein is divided and ligated. The physiological adhesion of the sigmoid colon to the lateral abdominal wall is mobilized.

The hypogastric nerve is exposed and protected as the posterior rectal space is separated. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and ligated at the root, followed by the division and ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein. The physiological adhesion of the sigmoid colon to the lateral abdominal wall is mobilized.

The surgeon incises the mesorectum ten centimeters away from the distal end of the tumor. A stapler is inserted to excise the rectum, followed by performing an end-to-end anastomosis using a twenty-nine circular stapler.
